explanations.11: The edx register holds the byte count of the string we want to print—we're telling the operating system how many characters to write.
explanations.12: We're loading the memory address (not the actual string content) of our 'hello' variable into ecx, which tells the system where to find the data to print.
explanations.14: In this old Linux calling convention, putting 4 in eax tells the operating system we want to use the sys_write function.
explanations.15: int 0x80 is like pressing a button that tells the CPU to stop normal execution and let the operating system handle our request using the values we just set up in those registers.
explanations.18: eax = 1 means we're asking the OS to run the sys_exit function, which shuts down our program.
explanations.19: This second interrupt 0x80 actually executes the exit call and terminates the program.
